title: An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say! (1)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, company inventory, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Legal allegations and findings remain attributed to the prosecution and courts described in the letter. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy. Page images 99-106 were used to remove a duplicated title fragment, restore the heavily corrupted asset-growth transition, recover the printed dates `7.9.1995` and `17.7.1991`, reconstruct damaged company names, and repair mechanical OCR joins. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: You may have read the opening line of a song: “An elegant hairdo, they say; fragrant screw-pine flowers, they say!” Only when you read the lines that follow will you understand what I mean and see its full significance clearly.

The details of Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case, the proceedings that lasted eighteen years and the final judgment are not matters you need to learn afresh from me. The people of the country understand them well. The case could be divided into chapters such as arrest, prison, punishment, conditional bail and interim release. Yet it would be good for you, and good for the country, to set it out fully. Truth may at least be established in the country's future, and this record may benefit and warn future generations. With that hope, after remaining silent for the past few days, I have begun this series of letters to explain the matter in some detail.

The title of this letter is “An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say!” Trusting that you will understand without my publishing its next line, “But inside, there are lice and nits, they say,” I begin this letter and shall continue it for another day or two.

The disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa and three others had been dragged through various stages for eighteen years. Judge John Michael D'Cunha of the Special Court in Bengaluru announced that he would deliver judgment on 20 September. The accused, citing their security, requested that judgment be delivered only on 27 September. The Judge accepted the request and delivered it on that date.

The judgment imposed a fine of Rs. 100 crore and four years' imprisonment on Jayalalithaa. Sasikala, Ilavarasi and V.N. Sudhakaran were each sentenced to four years' imprisonment and fined Rs. 10 crore. Judge John Michael D'Cunha set out the supporting evidence and testimony under the relevant legal provisions in his detailed 1,136-page judgment.

I expressed no opinion immediately after the judgment. This was an important case, and I thought it proper to remain cautious and patient, read the entire judgment carefully and then explain it. Nor was I prepared to rejoice merely because the AIADMK leader had been punished, or to grieve because she has now obtained conditional bail from the Supreme Court. Yet some AIADMK members claim that I somehow filed a false case against Jayalalithaa. What is the truth? This series is meant to share with you what the Bengaluru Special Court actually said in its judgment.
At the outset of his judgment, Judge John Michael D'Cunha explained why the disproportionate-assets case, though begun in Chennai, was transferred to Bengaluru.

Former Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, V.N. Sudhakaran and J. Ilavarasi were prosecuted for offences punishable under the Prevention of Corruption Act and Sections 120-B and 109 of the Indian Penal Code.

J. Jayalalithaa served as Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu from 24 June 1991 to 13 May 1996. Before that, she had acted in films during the 1960s. She was the daughter of the late N.R. Sandhya and, after her mother's death in 1971, was heir only to the properties of Natya Kala Niketan.

The second accused, Sasikala, was the wife of Natarajan, who had risen to the post of Deputy Director in the Tamil Nadu Government's Department of Information and Public Relations before resigning. Married to Natarajan in 1970, Sasikala had visited Jayalalithaa's home from time to time. After 1988 she became Jayalalithaa's companion described as a sister not born of the same mother, and lived with her in that house.

Jayalalithaa publicly accepted V.N. Sudhakaran, the son of Sasikala's sister Vanithamani and T.T. Vivekanandan, as her foster son. On 7 September 1995, she conducted his enormously extravagant marriage to Sathyalakshmi in Chennai.
The fourth accused, Ilavarasi, was the wife of Sasikala's elder brother V. Jayaraman. After Jayaraman's death, Ilavarasi too lived in Jayalalithaa's house from the beginning of 1992, as Sasikala did.

According to the prosecution, on 17 July 1991 Jayalalithaa and Sasikala possessed assets valued at Rs. 2,01,83,957, including Jaya Publications, Sasi Enterprises, *Namadhu MGR* and properties acquired in their names.
After Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister on 1 July 1991, the assets of Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Ilavarasi and Sudhakaran multiplied many times over. They included:

1. J. Farm Houses 2. J.S. Housing Development 3. J. Real Estate 4. Jaya Contractors and Builders 5. J.S. Leasing and Maintenance 6. Green Farm Houses 7. Metal King 8. Super Duper TV Limited 9. Anjaneya Printers Private Limited 10. Ramraj Agro Mills Limited 11. Signora Business Enterprises Private Limited 12. Lex Property Development Private Limited 13. Riverway Agro Products Private Limited 14. Meadow Agro Farms Private Limited 15. Indo Doha Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals Limited 16. A.B. Advertising Services 17. Vigneswara Builders 18. Lakshmi Constructions 19. Gopal Promoters 20. Sakthi Constructions 21. Namasivaya Housing Development 22. Ayyappa Property Development 23. C Enclave 24. Nava Sakthi Contractors and Builders 25. Asianic Constructions 26. Green Garden Apartments 27. Marble Marvels 28. Vinod Video Vision 29. Pax Universal 30. Fresh Mushrooms 31. Kodanad Tea Estate

Between 1 July 1991 and 30 April 1996, these companies carried on none of the ordinary activities expected of commercial businesses apart from acquiring land, machinery, buildings and other assets. No income-tax returns were filed through them, nor were sales-tax assessments made for them. Jayalalithaa herself filed no income-tax return from the 1987-88 year until November 1992.

After 1 July 1991, movable and immovable assets were purchased not only in Jayalalithaa's name but also in the names of the other three and of companies established in their names. Examination of the four accused's bank accounts showed frequent deposits into various accounts far beyond their known income. Money was repeatedly transferred from one account to another to acquire assets unlawfully. Since this occurred while Jayalalithaa was in office, and because Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Ilavarasi and Sudhakaran all lived in the same house, it was proved that the assets were in reality purchased by Jayalalithaa.

The prosecution further alleged that Jayalalithaa, a public servant, and her three associates entered into a criminal conspiracy and accumulated assets worth Rs. 66,65,20,395 in Jayalalithaa's name, in the others' names and in the names of their companies. These assets were disproportionate to their income.

According to the prosecution, their total income between 1 July 1991 and 30 April 1996, including rent, interest on bank accounts and other deposits, agricultural income received by Jayalalithaa and the other three, loans and her salary as Chief Minister, was Rs. 9,34,26,054.

During the same period, Jayalalithaa and the others were calculated to have spent Rs. 11,56,56,833 on interest on loans, loan repayments and other expenditure. Thus, as of 30 April 1996, Jayalalithaa, while a public servant, had acquired assets valued at Rs. 66,65,20,395 beyond her known income. This constituted an offence punishable under the Prevention of Corruption Act.

The case against Jayalalithaa began with a complaint submitted to the Governor of Tamil Nadu by Dr. Subramanian Swamy, then president of the Janata Party. On 14 June 1996, he filed a complaint before the Principal Sessions and Special Judge in Chennai alleging that Jayalalithaa had accumulated assets beyond her income after becoming Chief Minister. The Judge registered the complaint as a case and, on 21 June 1996, ordered senior IPS officer Lathika Saran to investigate and report within two months.

Lathika Saran obtained reports from various banks, registration offices and the Registrar of Companies and proceeded with the investigation. Jayalalithaa and Sasikala challenged the order in the Madras High Court, and the investigation was stayed for a short period by an order dated 14 August 1996. On 4 September 1996, the High Court itself lifted the stay and permitted the investigation to continue. Rejecting Jayalalithaa's request for anticipatory bail, the High Court ruled: “There is prima facie evidence supporting the corruption case against Jayalalithaa. It is incorrect to say that the case was filed because of political hostility.”

After the Madras High Court permitted the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption to investigate on 4 September 1996, its Director, V.C. Perumal, instructed Nallamma Naidu, then Additional Superintendent of Police in the Directorate, to investigate the case. V.C. Perumal, who was later promoted as Inspector General, filed the First Information Report against Jayalalithaa on 18 September 1996.

Nallamma Naidu began the investigation, searched Jayalalithaa's residences under warrants and collected a large body of testimony and evidence. On 22 January 1997, he applied to the Special Court for permission to add the other three as accused. After completing the investigation in accordance with procedure, the charge sheet against Jayalalithaa was filed and registered in the Special Court, the Additional Sessions Court in Chennai, on 4 June 1997.

On 5 June 1997, the Special Judge in Chennai examined the alleged offences and ordered summons to be issued to all four accused. At the request of the three accused other than Jayalalithaa, the Judge also ordered that they be given a Tamil translation of the charge sheet. On 21 October 1997, the Judge dismissed the accused persons' petitions for discharge and framed the charges.

Jayalalithaa's side challenged in the Madras High Court the establishment of the Special Court and the appointment of a Special Judge for the disproportionate-assets case. Since the trial arrangements had been made in conformity with the Constitution of India, their petition was dismissed. Their appeal to the Supreme Court was also dismissed on 14 May 1999 by Justices G.T. Nanavati and S.P. Kurdukar. It was thereby established that the Special Court and the appointment of its Judge were constitutionally valid.
With affection,
M.K.
20 October 2014
(To be continued)
